explanation.title: Making Decisions in Python with if, elif, and else
explanation.content:
# Making Decisions with Conditions

Every useful program must make decisions. Think of a traffic light: it checks the current colour and acts accordingly — green means go, yellow means slow down, red means stop. An `if` statement works the same way in Python: it checks a condition and runs a block of code only when that condition is `True`.

## The if Statement

```python
temperature = 38
if temperature > 37:
    print("You have a fever.")
```

Notice the two essential syntax rules:
1. The condition is followed by a **colon** `:`
2. The body is **indented** by four spaces (or one tab)

Python uses indentation to define which lines belong to the `if` block. This is different from many other languages that use curly braces.

## Adding an else Branch

```python
if temperature > 37:
    print("You have a fever.")
else:
    print("Your temperature is normal.")
```

The `else` block runs when the condition is `False`. It is optional — you do not always need one.

## Multiple Conditions with elif

When you have more than two possible outcomes, use `elif` (short for "else if"):

```python
score = 75
if score >= 90:
    print("Grade: A")
elif score >= 80:
    print("Grade: B")
elif score >= 70:
    print("Grade: C")
else:
    print("Grade: F")
```

Python evaluates conditions top to bottom and stops at the **first** one that is `True`. Once a match is found, all remaining `elif` and `else` branches are skipped.

## The Ternary Expression

For simple two-outcome decisions, Python offers a one-line shortcut:

```python
label = "Adult" if age >= 18 else "Minor"
```

This is equivalent to an `if/else` block but fits on a single line when both outcomes are short.

In the code example below, you will see a grade classifier that uses `if`, `elif`, and `else` to assign a letter grade and feedback message.

codeExample.title: Student Grade Classifier
codeExample.code:
# Student Grade Classifier
# Assigns a letter grade and feedback based on a numeric score

student = "Kiran"
score   = 82

if score >= 90:
    grade    = "A"
    feedback = "Outstanding performance!"
elif score >= 80:
    grade    = "B"
    feedback = "Great work, keep it up!"
elif score >= 70:
    grade    = "C"
    feedback = "Good effort, room to improve."
elif score >= 60:
    grade    = "D"
    feedback = "Needs more practice."
else:
    grade    = "F"
    feedback = "Please seek extra help."

print(f"Student  : {student}")
print(f"Score    : {score}/100")
print(f"Grade    : {grade}")
print(f"Feedback : {feedback}")

# Ternary expression example
status = "Pass" if score >= 50 else "Fail"
print(f"Status   : {status}")
codeExample.explanation:
- `if score >= 90:` — evaluates whether `score` is 90 or more; the colon is mandatory and the body must be indented
- `elif score >= 80:` — only evaluated when all previous conditions were `False`; stops at the first `True` match
- `grade = "B"` inside an `elif` block — any variable assigned inside a block is still accessible after the block ends
- `else:` — catches everything that did not match any `if` or `elif`; has no condition of its own
- `"Pass" if score >= 50 else "Fail"` — the ternary expression; the value before `if` is chosen when the condition is `True`

exercise.title: Write a Ticket Price Calculator
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that calculates the ticket price for a cinema based on the customer's age. Define a variable age (integer). Apply these rules: under 5 is free (price = 0), age 5 to 12 pays 100, age 13 to 17 pays 150, age 18 to 59 pays 200, and age 60 or above pays 120. Print the age and the ticket price on separate labelled lines.

Test with age = 25. Expected output:
Age: 25
Ticket Price: 200
exercise.starterCode:
# Cinema Ticket Price Calculator

age = 25

# Use if, elif, and else to set ticket_price
if age < 5:
    ticket_price = 0
elif age <= 12:
    ticket_price = 100
# Your code here for ages 13-17
# Your code here for ages 18-59
# Your code here for 60 and above

print("Age:",          age)
print("Ticket Price:", ticket_price)
hints: 💡 Think about the order — check from the smallest age range upward so earlier conditions eliminate lower ranges.
💡 For ages 13 to 17, write elif age <= 17: because earlier branches already ruled out ages below 13.
💡 The last range (60 and above) does not need a condition — use else: since all lower ages were already handled.

questions.question: What is the correct syntax to start an if statement in Python?
options: if (condition) {
if condition:
if condition then
IF condition:
questions.explanation: Python's if statement syntax is: if condition: — no parentheses around the condition (they are optional but not required), a colon at the end, and the body indented on the next line. Python is case-sensitive, so IF would cause a NameError. There are no curly braces in Python; indentation defines the block.

questions.question: When does the else block of an if/elif/else chain execute?
options: Always, regardless of the conditions above it
Only when the first if condition is False
When none of the if or elif conditions evaluated to True
When every condition in the chain evaluated to True
questions.explanation: The else block runs only when every if and elif condition above it evaluated to False. If any if or elif condition is True, Python executes that block and then skips the rest of the chain including else. The else has no condition of its own — it acts as the default fallback.

questions.question: What is the output of: label = "Yes" if 10 > 5 else "No" followed by print(label)?
options: No
True
Yes
questions.explanation: The ternary expression evaluates the condition 10 > 5, which is True. When the condition is True, the expression returns the value before the if keyword — which is "Yes". So label is assigned "Yes" and print(label) displays Yes.

explanation.title: Comparing Values and Combining Conditions
explanation.content:
# Comparison Operators

A condition in an `if` statement always evaluates to `True` or `False`. The most direct way to produce this result is with a **comparison operator**, which compares two values:

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5` | `True` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `True` |
| `>` | Greater than | `7 > 3` | `True` |
| `<` | Less than | `3 < 7` | `True` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal | `5 >= 5` | `True` |
| `<=` | Less than or equal | `4 <= 3` | `False` |

**Critical distinction:** `=` assigns a value; `==` compares two values. Confusing them is one of the most common bugs beginners write.

## Logical Operators

To combine multiple conditions, use the three logical operators:

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `and` | Both must be `True` | `age >= 18 and has_id` |
| `or` | At least one must be `True` | `is_admin or is_owner` |
| `not` | Reverses `True`/`False` | `not is_banned` |

### Truth tables

| A | B | A and B | A or B |
|---|---|---|---|
| True | True | True | True |
| True | False | False | True |
| False | True | False | True |
| False | False | False | False |

## The in Operator

The `in` operator checks membership — whether a value exists inside a collection or string:

```python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "mango"]
if "mango" in fruits:
    print("Mango is available!")

if "@" in email:
    print("Email looks valid.")
```

## Chaining Comparisons

Python allows a readable shortcut for range checks:

```python
if 18 <= age <= 60:    # same as age >= 18 and age <= 60
    print("Working age group")
```

In the code example below, you will see logical operators and comparison operators combined to build a login validator.

codeExample.title: Login Access Validator
codeExample.code:
# Login Access Validator — uses comparison and logical operators

username    = "rahul_dev"
password    = "secret123"
login_count = 3
is_banned   = False

# Correct credentials (case-sensitive)
correct_user = "rahul_dev"
correct_pass = "secret123"
max_attempts = 5

# Check all conditions
valid_credentials = (username == correct_user) and (password == correct_pass)
within_limit      = login_count <= max_attempts
account_active    = not is_banned

print("Credentials OK :", valid_credentials)
print("Within limit   :", within_limit)
print("Account active :", account_active)

if valid_credentials and within_limit and account_active:
    print("\nAccess GRANTED — welcome!", username)
elif not account_active:
    print("\nAccess DENIED — account is banned.")
elif not within_limit:
    print("\nAccess DENIED — too many attempts.")
else:
    print("\nAccess DENIED — incorrect username or password.")
codeExample.explanation:
- `username == correct_user` — the `==` operator compares two string values; a single `=` would assign, not compare
- `(username == correct_user) and (password == correct_pass)` — `and` requires both comparisons to be `True`
- `not is_banned` — the `not` operator flips `False` to `True`, making `account_active` `True` when the account is not banned
- `valid_credentials and within_limit and account_active` — all three boolean variables must be `True` for access to be granted
- `not account_active` in the `elif` — shorthand for `account_active == False`; `not` is cleaner and more readable

exercise.title: Build a Loan Eligibility Checker
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that checks loan eligibility. Use three variables: age (integer), monthly_income (float), and has_existing_loan (boolean). Rules: age must be 21-60 inclusive, monthly_income must be at least 25000, and has_existing_loan must be False. Print "Eligible for loan" if all rules are met, otherwise print "Not eligible".

Test: age=30, monthly_income=35000.0, has_existing_loan=False.
Expected: Eligible for loan
exercise.starterCode:
# Loan Eligibility Checker

age               = 30
monthly_income    = 35000.0
has_existing_loan = False

# Check all three conditions together using and
# Age: 21 to 60 inclusive (use chained comparison)
# Income: at least 25000
# No existing loan: has_existing_loan must be False

if 21 <= age <= 60 and monthly_income >= 25000 and not has_existing_loan:
    print("Eligible for loan")
else:
    print("Not eligible")
exercise.solutionCode:
age               = 30
monthly_income    = 35000.0
has_existing_loan = False

if 21 <= age <= 60 and monthly_income >= 25000 and not has_existing_loan:
    print("Eligible for loan")
else:
    print("Not eligible")
hints: 💡 Use a chained comparison for the age range: 21 <= age <= 60 is cleaner than two separate conditions joined with and.
💡 Check income with monthly_income >= 25000 and combine it with the age check using and.
💡 Use not has_existing_loan to check that no existing loan exists — this reads as 'does not have an existing loan'.

questions.question: What is the difference between = and == in Python?
options: They are identical and can be used interchangeably
= compares two values; == assigns a value to a variable
= assigns a value to a variable; == compares two values
= is used for integers; == is used for strings
questions.explanation: = is the assignment operator — it stores a value in a variable: x = 5. == is the equality comparison operator — it checks whether two values are equal and returns True or False: x == 5. Using = inside an if condition is a SyntaxError in Python (unlike some other languages where it is a silent bug).

questions.question: What does the expression True and False evaluate to?
options: True
False
None
Error
questions.explanation: The and operator returns True only when both operands are True. Since False is on the right side, True and False evaluates to False. If you think of and as 'both must agree', one False is enough to make the whole expression False.

questions.question: What does 18 <= age <= 60 mean in Python?
options: age is exactly 18 or exactly 60
age is greater than 18 but less than 60
age is at least 18 and at most 60 — a chained comparison
This is a syntax error in Python
questions.explanation: Python supports chained comparisons, so 18 <= age <= 60 is valid and is equivalent to age >= 18 and age <= 60. Python evaluates both halves and requires both to be True. This syntax is more readable than the equivalent written with and, so it is widely used for range checks.

explanation.title: Nested Conditions and Python's match Statement
explanation.content:
# Nested if Statements

Sometimes a decision depends on another decision. Imagine a vending machine: it first checks whether you inserted enough money, and only if you did, it then checks which button you pressed. This layered logic is called **nested conditions** — an `if` statement inside another `if` statement.

```python
balance = 50
item    = "chips"

if balance >= 10:
    if item == "chips":
        print("Dispensing chips.")
        balance -= 10
    elif item == "soda":
        print("Dispensing soda.")
        balance -= 15
    else:
        print("Unknown item.")
else:
    print("Insufficient balance.")
```

Each level of nesting adds four spaces of indentation. Python uses indentation to determine which `if` owns which `elif`/`else`.

## When to Avoid Deep Nesting

Nesting beyond two levels makes code hard to read. A common technique to reduce nesting is the **early return** or **guard clause** pattern — check failure conditions first and exit early:

```python
# Deep nesting (harder to read)
if is_logged_in:
    if has_permission:
        if not is_banned:
            process_request()

# Guard clauses (easier to read)
if not is_logged_in:   return
if not has_permission: return
if is_banned:          return
process_request()
```

## The match Statement (Python 3.10+)

Python 3.10 introduced `match`, a cleaner alternative to long `if/elif` chains when you are matching a value against a fixed set of possibilities:

```python
day_number = 3

match day_number:
    case 1: print("Monday")
    case 2: print("Tuesday")
    case 3: print("Wednesday")
    case 4: print("Thursday")
    case 5: print("Friday")
    case _: print("Weekend")   # _ is the default case
```

The `case _:` block (underscore) is the wildcard — it matches anything that did not match an earlier case, equivalent to `else`.

## match with Multiple Values per Case

```python
match status_code:
    case 200 | 201 | 204:
        print("Success")
    case 400 | 422:
        print("Client error")
    case 500 | 502 | 503:
        print("Server error")
    case _:
        print("Unknown code")
```

The `|` operator inside a `case` matches any of several values. In the code example below, you will see nested conditions and a `match` statement applied to a simple ATM simulator.

codeExample.title: Simple ATM Transaction Simulator
codeExample.code:
# ATM Transaction Simulator — nested conditions + match

pin_correct  = True
balance      = 5000
transaction  = "withdraw"   # options: withdraw, deposit, balance
amount       = 1500

# Outer condition: pin must be correct before anything
if pin_correct:
    match transaction:
        case "withdraw":
            if amount <= 0:
                print("Invalid amount.")
            elif amount > balance:
                print("Insufficient funds.")
            else:
                balance -= amount
                print(f"Withdrew: {amount}")
                print(f"Remaining balance: {balance}")
        case "deposit":
            if amount <= 0:
                print("Invalid deposit amount.")
            else:
                balance += amount
                print(f"Deposited: {amount}")
                print(f"New balance: {balance}")
        case "balance":
            print(f"Current balance: {balance}")
        case _:
            print("Unknown transaction type.")
else:
    print("Incorrect PIN. Access denied.")
codeExample.explanation:
- `if pin_correct:` — the outer guard; nothing inside runs unless the PIN is correct
- `match transaction:` — evaluates the string in `transaction` and jumps to the matching `case`
- `case "withdraw":` — matches when `transaction` equals the string `"withdraw"` exactly
- `elif amount > balance:` — nested inside the `"withdraw"` case; only reached when `amount > 0`
- `balance -= amount` — the augmented subtraction operator; shorthand for `balance = balance - amount`
- `case _:` — the wildcard default; runs when no other case matched

exercise.title: Build a Restaurant Menu Order System
exercise.instructions:
Write a menu ordering program. Define two variables: category (a string: 'food', 'drink', or 'dessert') and item (a string). Use a match statement on category. Inside each case, use nested if/elif to check the item name and set a price. For food: pizza=250, burger=150. For drink: cola=50, juice=80. For dessert: icecream=90, cake=120. Default case and unknown items should print 'Not available'. Print the item and its price.

Test: category='food', item='pizza'. Expected output: pizza: 250
exercise.starterCode:
# Restaurant Menu Order System

category = "food"
item     = "pizza"
price    = 0

match category:
    case "food":
        if item == "pizza":
            price = 250
        elif item == "burger":
            price = 150
        else:
            price = -1    # -1 means not available
    # Your code here for case "drink" (cola=50, juice=80)
    # Your code here for case "dessert" (icecream=90, cake=120)
    case _:
        price = -1

if price == -1:
    print("Not available")
else:
    print(f"{item}: {price}")
exercise.solutionCode:
category = "food"
item     = "pizza"
price    = 0

match category:
    case "food":
        if   item == "pizza":   price = 250
        elif item == "burger":  price = 150
        else:                   price = -1
    case "drink":
        if   item == "cola":    price = 50
        elif item == "juice":   price = 80
        else:                   price = -1
    case "dessert":
        if   item == "icecream": price = 90
        elif item == "cake":     price = 120
        else:                    price = -1
    case _:
        price = -1

if price == -1:
    print("Not available")
else:
    print(f"{item}: {price}")
hints: 💡 Add case "drink": followed by indented if/elif blocks for cola and juice, using the same pattern as the food case.
💡 Add case "dessert": the same way, with if/elif for icecream and cake, and an else that sets price = -1.
💡 The final if price == -1 check handles both unknown categories and unknown items within a known category.

questions.question: In Python's match statement, what does case _ mean?
options: It matches only the value None
It matches only empty strings
It is the wildcard default case that matches any value not caught above
It causes a SyntaxError because _ is not a valid case value
questions.explanation: In a Python match statement, case _: is the wildcard pattern — the underscore matches any value that was not matched by any earlier case. It works like else in an if/elif chain. If no case _ is present and no case matches, the match statement simply does nothing.

questions.question: What version of Python introduced the match statement?
options: Python 2.7
Python 3.6
Python 3.8
Python 3.10
questions.explanation: The match statement (structural pattern matching) was introduced in Python 3.10. If you run code containing match on Python 3.9 or earlier, you will get a SyntaxError. For older Python versions, use a chain of if/elif statements to achieve the same effect.

questions.question: What is the main risk of deeply nested if statements?
options: Python raises an error when nesting goes deeper than two levels
Deeply nested code becomes difficult to read and maintain
Nested if statements run slower than flat if/elif chains
Variables defined inside inner if blocks are deleted after the block
questions.explanation: Python does not limit nesting depth, but deeply nested code is hard to read, test, and maintain. Each new level of indentation makes it harder to track which else belongs to which if. The professional solution is to use guard clauses — check failure conditions early and handle them at the top of the function — keeping the main logic at the lowest indentation level.
